paragraphs.body: It was a hot summer day in the backseat of a Jeep, all huddled up with my sister, mom, aunts, and uncle. The drive was long and bumpy, on a deserted road with no clear path ahead. Dust rose in the air as the wheels moved, leaving an orangish cloud behind. The weather was hot, but the wind coming through the rolled-down windows kept it at bay. During the spring season, my mom was taking us to visit her older sister in Khanaqin, a city in the Garmian region and part of Kurdish territory, to celebrate Nowruz, the festival marking the Kurdish New Year. Although my family has never been particularly celebratory, we always light wood in the spring. The smell of burning wood brings back memories and feelings that I can’t put into words, except that it feels like home. Being born Kurdish, I take pride not only in my heritage and culture but also in my dialect and the history of my people. Although keeping my culture and language alive is important to me, there is friction between my Kurdish and American identities. These differences spark discussions around topics such as the contrast between individualism in the U.S. and collectivism in Kurdish culture, struggles with self-identity, and differences in communication between macro and micro languages.

annotations.text: These differences spark discussions around topics such as the contrast between individualism in the U.S. and collectivism in Kurdish culture, struggles with self-identity, and differences in communication between macro and micro languages.
annotations.comment: This sounds like a thesis statement for a five-paragraph essay! Don't fall back on that, especially after a great intro :)

paragraphs.body: Growing up in the United States, I found stories of friends needing to pay their parents' rent when they turned eighteen baffling. Even the idea of moving out and paying for college contrasted sharply with the ideas I grew up with. In my culture, people often live with their parents even after marriage and starting a family. There is a strong cultural emphasis on staying connected with parents and family members in many Kurdish families, placing individual desires in the background. In contrast, American culture has a huge focus on individualism, centering on personal freedom, success, and dreams that do not revolve around family but rather highlight the self as the primary interest. Growing up in a collective culture, my parents would have our neighbors over and send us with plates of food for them. Sharing food is common where I grew up, especially during the holidays. Funerals are another occasion where food is shared with grieving families. "They need to have the energy to grieve," my mom would say. Grieving and celebrating are collective experiences shared by everyone. In contrast, I experienced a more individualistic society in the West, where the focus is on personal dreams, goals, and desires. My parents always wanted me to pursue a career in pharmaceuticals or engineering, despite my passion for the arts. Exposure to individualism allowed me to seek my personal dreams, instead of prioritizing my parents' goals above mine. To many people who grew up in cultures emphasizing family and community first, as well as viewing elders’ words as gospel, prioritizing one’s own needs is unthinkable.

annotations.text: In contrast, American culture has a huge focus on individualism, centering on personal freedom, success, and dreams that do not revolve around family but rather highlight the self as the primary interest. Growing up in a collective culture, my parents would have our neighbors over and send us with plates of food for them.
annotations.comment: interesting. Let's talk about this collective v individual mindset!

paragraphs.body: To live in the East means knowing: There is us, but no Me. To live in the West means knowing: There is me, but no home.

annotations.text: To live in the East means knowing: There is us, but no Me. To live in the West means knowing: There is me, but no home.

paragraphs.body: “Era emaé emrekeiamen?”My parents used to always say that to me. They always said that when I said or did something that went against traditional values. If I asked my parents to stay out late, they would say “Era emaé emrekeiamen?” My answer would always be no, even though I am American. I would reply, No, we are not Americans, but it’s not the 1970s in Iraq, and things are different now. Although my parents and I speak the same Kurdish dialect, Garmiani, we don’t always understand each other fully. They spoke as two Kurdish children who were Internally Displaced Persons (IDP), while I spoke as someone who is half-American and half-Kurdish. My parents grew up in the Garmiyan region, known for its dry and warm climate in the countryside. They were very young when the government displaced them to central Iraq, leaving them to navigate the school system, language barriers, and cultural differences without any prior understanding of Arabic. This displacement made them even more attached to and proud of their heritage. Ironically, I also experienced a form of displacement, growing up in a different country with a new language and culture. My mom taught me Arabic when I was five so I wouldn’t struggle in school, but in the U.S., I had to teach myself English and then teach it to my parents. Reflecting on this, my parents probably did the same for their parents. Even though the Kurds are stateless, my parents always told us to say we were Kurds first, then name our country. My parents wanted my sister and me to take pride in both our Kurdish heritage and our American birth. My parents are Kurds born in Iraq; I am a Kurd born in Iraq but grew up in America.

annotations.text: My mom taught me Arabic when I was five so I wouldn’t struggle in school, but in the U.S., I had to teach myself English and then teach it to my parents. My parents wanted my sister and me to take pride in both our Kurdish heritage and our American birth. My parents are Kurds born in Iraq; I am a Kurd born in Iraq but grew up in America.

paragraphs.body: To speak Kurdish means knowing: The pain of losing home. To speak English means knowing: Home is where you speak the same language.

annotations.text: To speak English means knowing: Home is where you speak the same language.
annotations.comment: What do you mean? Let's explore.

paragraphs.body: Meeting another Kurdish person is quite hard, but meeting one who speaks the same dialect as yours is even harder. The dialects are all Kurdish, yet it’s hard to communicate clearly with someone who speaks Kurmanji as a Garmiani speaker. Kurdish is a language with many roots but communication is hard between them. Compared to the widely big English language that is spoken all over the world, Kurdish is a small language that is only spoken in small parts of the globe. You can communicate with almost everyone in the world in English, but it’s like finding a needle in a mountain of hay to find one that speaks home to me. Because Kurds are so spread out regionally and linguistically it creates a sense of us against the other. The bigger dialects assume smaller dialects as not Kurdish enough or simply not Kurdish at all. This is a sentiment I have experienced here in the U.S. from another Kurdish girl who came straight from Kurdistan. I have never been to Kurdistan, but I still take off my shoes before going inside my house, I still eat Tarhana in the winter, have Mashkae from a goat stomach, and I still speak Kurdish. Qi ushiden mae chaem? (who decides what I am?) Era mae Kurdiniem? (am I not Kurdish?)

annotations.text: (who decides what I am?) Era mae Kurdiniem? (am I not Kurdish?)
annotations.comment: Ah! Look at that. You're asking the same questions I'm asking!

paragraphs.body: When we arrived in Khanaqin, the wind was cool, the sky a clear blue, and the grass greener than I had ever seen. It was midday when we reached my aunt’s house. I still remember the orange and yellow brick walls of her gate. The whole neighborhood spiraled up a hill, with narrow, pebble-strewn roads and greenery everywhere. At the neighborhood entrance, fields stretched out in a beautiful green shade for miles. There was a fireplace with chairs and pillows to the side, and in the distance, soccer goals dotted the field. As the sun set, the sky shifted from blue to shades of yellow and orange, like flames engulfing the wood. The smell of burning wood, fresh grass, and food cooking nearby is so vivid in my memory. I hold that memory close to my dilm (heart). Every spring, I burn a branch of wood and watch it burn at sunset. They say it burns away all that is ill and brings warmth and togetherness. My sister once smelled it and said it made her feel nostalgic for something she couldn’t quite name. Perhaps home and culture are not about what surrounds us but rather what we carry within.

annotations.text: Perhaps home and culture are not about what surrounds us but rather what we carry within.
annotations.comment: Ah yes. And how is what you carry within related to lighting wood?

paragraphs.body: “Burning Wood smells like home, red and hot like the blood in my veins. We might not remember it but unconsciously know it.”

annotations.text: “Burning Wood smells like home, red and hot like the blood in my veins. We might not remember it but unconsciously know it.”
